sentences.text: Yes.
Lohamei ha-Geta'ot.
And this is the Fighters' Museum.
And they-- they-- some of the pamphlets were delivered there by some of the underground people that managed to survive from our group, and not many.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am sorry to say that a very great percentage of the people that were fortunate to join the underground perished, too.
I don't know whether 5% survived from the underground fighters.
And talking about the underground fighters, the contact person that came to us, not just brought us the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] underground newspaper, but we also knew what was happening around.
And in 1941, the end of it, and in the beginning of 1942, there were already happening the mass murders in Chelmno.
And they were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] collecting the Jews in buses in the [INAUDIBLE].
And so, anyhow, the situation started being very, very grave.
And there was no tomorrow.
The Germans weren't anymore the cultural people like they were looked upon through our eyes and the eyes of all European people.
They let themselves be led, blind, by their Fuhrer.
And they had a blind allegiance.
And in 1941, on the Wannsee Conference, they decided to liquidate all the Jews.
And they were hoping that there will be nobody to tell about their story of crime and inhumanity.
I remember one evening I dared-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] across the street, I-- where we lived, I had my very closest girlfriend.
And I thought, it is just such a short way, I'm going to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take my chances.
You know, when you are young you take chances.
I ran over.
I succeeded to run over.
But we were walking in the courtyard by my girlfriend, and a German saw us.
He was walking on the street, and he came in, and he-- he grabbed me.
He grabbed me by the hair, and he kicked me in my throat.
And I thought, at that time, that I am not going-- with his boots and everything.
And I thought, I'm not going to make it.
But evidently, I was meant to live.
And this was one of the shaking experiences in the ghetto.
There were other things.
Let's see.
As I mentioned before, the Germans used to round up certain people and shoot them.
And one night was a very bad night of shooting.
And I remember my old grandmother, she was already in her late 70s or early 80s.
At the time, they don't talk about the age.
And I remember, in the middle of the night, we had some carrots that we must have bought in-- you know-- and my grandmother was grating the carrots to camouflage the shooting.
And it was-- and the next morning, we got up.
And around the streets there were leaders of the community lying there, dead.
And-- and these-- these were-- you just felt very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] persecuted, because you couldn't go anyplace.
You couldn't-- I couldn't move around.
The only thing I did in the beginning of the closed ghetto, I attended, together with two other girlfriends, I attended-- this was the end of 1939 and 1940.
I attended a school of sewing and modeling.
This was out of the ghetto.
That lady, she was a very fine lady.
She had some connections with the German.
And she needed the students and the-- the money for the tuition.
And she gave us permits to come for those classes.
And since this was, like, a work re-education, they thought they never know when they are going to be able to use seamstresses or so on, so they let us go there.
And that was a double bonus.
Not only did I learn to sew-- and I remember, we didn't even sew on materials, we sewed tissue paper.
Because-- and we cut the tissue paper.
But I was able to go there and learn the basics of sewing.
And we also were passing [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of the ghetto.
She was a very fine Gentile woman.
She gave us some money, and she bought us some food.
And we were trying to smuggle it in, a little bit, the best we could, without bringing suspicion to us and trouble to her.
And we-- while we were there, she fed us, too.
So that was a very, very unusual break for me and my two girlfriends.
Maria-- she was one of those silent stars in the sky of Gentile people that gave a hand to the Jewish suffering.
Maria.
And I forgot her family name.
I wish I wouldn't.
She lived on [INAUDIBLE], which was one of the main streets in the city.
She had a very nice facility.
Before the war, she was a very prominent modeling school.
And during the war, she could use more students.
So she was alone, not in such a good shape.
But she had some connections.
Whether she had a friend, a German, or whatever it is.
And she managed to get those permits for us.
Pardon?
All through 1940, when the ghetto was already closed. 1940 to the beginning of 1941.
And then it all ended.
Jews couldn't go, on any permits, out.
All the permits ended.
In fact, my father was in such a situation.
As I mentioned, that was my father and my brother work for a [NON-ENGLISH].
And-- and he used to send them to Piotrkow, where the factories were, to take the orders, and so on.
And my brotherly usually went.
And he had perfect Aryan looks.
He had blue-grey eyes and blond hair.
And he walked out of the ghetto.
And immediately, he took off the armband.
We were wearing white armbands and a blue star of David-- Magen David was on them.
You couldn't go anyplace.
All the people in the ghetto were Jews, but we still had to wear those armbands, to degrade us.

So, not that it is a degradation to wear a Star of David, but the way we wore-- wore them, to point out that we are Jews, and the Germans thought that we are equal to vermin, not to a people that brought to the world good things-- like the face of one god, the 10 Commandments that is the moral code for humanity.
And comparative to the population that we were in each country, we had very wonderful cultural contributions.
But this was never pointed out.
It was pointed out-- things that were untrue and-- and things that-- we were used as scapegoats.
And those armbands we had to wear like an armband of shame.
So if we go out, right away, they should notice us.
And if they caught Jews out there--
Let's see.
My brother had a permission to go out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he was supposed to wear the armband, so people could see a mile away that he is a Jew on a permit.
But if you took it off, and he was noticed-- recognized by somebody-- right away, he would have been shot.
My father was very worried when he heard from somebody that he's taking off the armband once he goes out of the ghetto.
So he said, next time the [NON-ENGLISH] is going to send somebody to Piotrkow, I am going to go.
So he went.
And that was the beginning of 1940.
And a new law came out, whereby Jews couldn't travel, permit or no permit.
He was 30 kilometers away from our home, and he couldn't come home.
Because he would have taken his life immediately in his hand.
He had-- this was his home town, originally.
And he-- Piotrkow?
Piotrkow.
And he had brothers and sisters.
And, of course, he-- he lived there in-- in-- in a rooming place.
It was very-- a great hardship for our family to be separated from my father.
He was kind of like a shining star in our family.
A man that was-- [SPEAKING HEBREW], which means 'a man of joy and a man-- man of good deeds.' It-- it was just very hard not to see him and not to have a hope for him to come home.
Through the [NON-ENGLISH].
That he-- and this was a law, that he couldn't come home.
The law came out when he was there, and there was no-- you cannot-- you couldn't-- even the [NON-ENGLISH] could do nothing.
Because this was a general law, and nobody could break it.
So that was very, very rough for us.
And as I mentioned, when the news came in from the underground, when our contact came, they decided that it is time to join-- to smuggle out from the ghetto.
And they supply the Aryan papers for the people that they choose to take, that their looks were good, and so on.
I was fortunate to get the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Aryan papers in my group.
And this was in the middle of August 1942, when already the killing was going go on, and the death camps were being built.
We knew the news.
But if we were telling them to our people in the ghetto, to my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the older generation and so on, they wanted us to shush up!
They didn't believe it!
They thought that this is a wild imagination!
That the Germans could never do such a thing!
That these are rumors!
They couldn't believe it!
And this-- and don't make any panic, and don't tell lies.
I couldn't take them there and show them.
Because I had the right sources.
These were my friends in the underground that came and told us.
And why would they lie?
I believed it.
And we used to be a Zionist organization based on scouting, with very great ideals of helping people, of caring.
And, all of a sudden, we changed our values.
We wanted to join the underground and take revenge for whatever was happening to our brothers and-- and-- and-- and the Jewish people.
We didn't go out there-- The objective wasn't to go to hide.
The object-- and to live over the wall.

The objective was to go and do what we can-- fight the rail trains, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kill whoever you could that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hurtful, especially to the Jewish people.
And even some of the Jewish policemen were-- if they did not behave right, they were also-- they were under sentence of death from the group.
If they denounced people or something, or certain Poles, that they denounced people.
These were-- It was ridiculous to say that we could undertake to fight the Germans as an army.
We didn't have enough resources.
We didn't have-- there wasn't enough [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ammunition.
There were just a few guns.
And what they did is make bombs, the Molotov cocktails or the other things.
Even the people from the, later on, Warsaw ghetto uprising, were the nucleus of the [SPEAKING HEBREW]-- of the fighting-- halutz.
They were the people from one town to the other.
That they got organized the best way they could.
So, when my time came to go, I went.
I still cannot understand how my mother let me go.
Not only did-- did she let me go, she kind of pushed me.
She said, go.
Maybe you will survive.
So I left my mother, brother, and grandmother, and I smuggled out.
My mother gave me a little celluloid tube.
Needles used to be held in them.
It-- it was-- you could close it.
It was the size of my finger, here.
And there she put some valuable stones that were in the family.
And since we had contact with the drugstores, because they were buying medicine bottles from us, my brother could get me a poison pill.
And that's what he gave me as a goodbye present.
Should I be caught, I shouldn't suffer too.
Because the German was-- were known about their investigation and their beatings and their sadism, and so on-- that life was worse than death.
So this is what I went out from.
But I had one determination before I joined the underground.
And before-- when I said goodbye to my family and to my friends-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with a very heavy heart, because I didn't know whether I'm going to ever see them again.
I remember the leader from my organization, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Izaak.
He-- the evening when I said goodbye, I said, how will I ever be able to live without my [SPEAKING HEBREW], without my group, and without my family?
So he said something that stuck with me through life.
He said, [SPEAKING HEBREW], which means 'There is nothing so dear and near in life without what and whom life couldn't go on.' So he said, go.
And, sure enough, I took a permit from that junk dealer for my father, and I was hoping to help him with it.
Because the junk dealers could go five kilometers' radius out of the ghetto to collect the junk, and I thought this is going to help him.
And Those people were my neighbors.
They knew me well, and then we compensated them for it.
And I took it.
And I smuggled in, into the Piotrkow ghetto, all those things.
Smuggling in and out, this was taking your life very much in your hand.
But listen, this was our life.
And no other way was there to function.
My name in my Aryan paper was Jadwiga Stryjkowska, which was very close to my original name.
Jadwiga is this J-A-D-W-I-G-A.
They used to call me Jadzia at my home town, and Jadwiga is kind of extended.
And Stryjkowska is S-T-R-Y-J-- the J was added, and it sounded a little-- well, Stryjkowska is an Aryan name, too.
It was S-T-R-Y-J-K-O-W-S-K-A.
I choose that name so close to my original name because I had a diploma from the sewing school on my name, Jadzia Strykowska.
And that was so much alike that one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] letter could have been just an omission.
And I was hoping on Aryan paper maybe that it's going to give me a-- a possibility to do something and be-- help with something or earn something.
So.
And that was like another identification, too.
This was to support my papers.
And I smuggled into the ghetto and gave my-- to the Piotrkow ghetto-- and gave my father those [NON-ENGLISH] papers, which is the junk-collectors' papers.
And we had a great reunion.
We-- it was almost close to a year when I didn't see him.
And I was extremely, extremely fond of my parents, especially somehow of my father.
I was his little girl when I was still 17, you would think.
And at that time, in 1942, I was approaching 17.
It was close before the High Holidays.

And since we haven't seen each other, and since we didn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] whether we will ever see each other again, he kind of insisted that I should wait with him over the High Holidays.
And I kind of felt this wasn't so much to ask.
And I alone wanted it, too.
And we spent-- excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We spent very special holidays together, like-- realizing that this might be it.
The last time.
Everything kind of was like the last experience.
The Rosh Hashanah and the Yom Kippur, I remember.
[SOBS] And my father was attached there to a rabbi, too, that he prayed in a special shtiebel.
Even before the war, he used to go there with my brother for the High Holidays.
And he used to say-- talk with the rabbi how what can help and what we can do, and what we should do.
So the rabbi-- he was a very wonderful, realistic, smart man.
He says, Joseph, I cannot help myself.
How can I help you?
We just have to trust-- maybe tomorrow the Mashiach will come.
So the Jews never gave up the hope.
And the Mashiach was--?
Pardon?
The Mashiach was the-- the-- the prophet-- the Messiah.
And the Jews believe in the coming of the Messiah, when the lion is going to be able to live with the lamb, and when there is not going to be a war, when the humanity is going to reign all over.
And-- and that was the hope.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But, instead of it, early in the morning, after Yom Kippur, the Day of Atonement, the ghetto was surrounded.
And I got caught there.
My father-- there was a deportation.
My father had a permit that I bought him.
And when we were going through the selection, he was told to stay, and I-- since I had illegal papers-- I was still illegal, to begin with-- I wasn't going to show them.
Right away, they directed me to go to the transports out from the ghetto.
And they had rows of five [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] cattle wagons waiting there for the people on the Umschlagplatz, which was the-- the place of segregation of Jews for the deportation.
And I remember staying in a row, pretty close to the wagons.
Then I ran away to another row, a little farther away.
There were my aunts and uncles.
I could see my cousins.
And everybody said, Jadzia, come, you go with us.
Jadzia, come, you go with us.
Because people were told that this is resettlement.
That someplace else is going to be more food and maybe place to work and a possibility to survive.
But I knew different.
I knew that this is going to destruction.
So I kept working my ways all the way away from those cars.

And there were, at one point, dividing that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that platz, that Umschlagplatz, where they had people that are supposed to stay, and, on the other side, the people that were supposed to be sent away.
There were air-raid dugouts.
Just dugouts.
Holes in the ground.
And the people were going there with the permission of the Germans and the Werkschutz-- a lot of Ukrainians helped the Germans in the deportation of the Jews, and they were the guards.
And they were watching, and they were very mean.
So you could go in there and relieve your body needs.
And I took off my rucksack.
I-- I was prepared to go, so I had three pairs of clothes underneath, and I had my rucksack, and I had a pleated raincoat that I was wearing.
And I took it up, and I went into that hole.
And, instead of going out one side, I ran out to the other side, where my father was.
And there was a big commotion, and they were trying to find me.
And I threw off my raincoat.
And I, right away, positioned myself there and looked around where the girl is that ran away, pretending that I am looking for the girl, too.
And that gave me an opportunity to go in, in that small group, to the ghetto.
